artifacts.content:
Erin,

I’m sitting here in the driveway at 1413 Spruce, finally calming down after the last couple of weeks, and I just need to get this all out.

First, I still can’t shake that January 17 scare with Lauren in Unit 23. Coming back from the County Highway 3 site, heading down Washington by Brainerd High on the way to her 3:15 counseling at Essentia–Brainerd Clinic, the steering locked for a second and yanked us into the slush. We didn’t hit anything, but she went straight into a panic episode. Given how long Dr. Melissa Porter has been working with her on the anxiety since last fall, I feel awful that a city truck triggered it.

It’s just one more thing on top of everything with my dad. I keep thinking about his December 10 crash by Gregory Park on Laurel Street, sliding into that streetlight pole in the snow. We got called out at 11:45 p.m. to set barricades, and because Unit 23’s frame and brake lines are such a mess, we couldn’t tow the message board or salt spreader. We lost almost 40 minutes waiting on another truck while he and the tow truck sat there.

I also keep replaying Lauren’s fender-bender in the east lot by Brainerd High back on January 12, when Mark rushed out in Unit 23 and couldn’t even get the rear warning lights to work on Washington Street. Between that near-miss with the distracted driver, Dad’s crash by Gregory, and today’s steering stunt, it’s obvious that thing is a liability.

I know you’re tired of hearing about trucks, but this is why I’m so fixated on getting the replacement done before spring. Let’s still try to walk the Paul Bunyan trail Sunday if the weather holds; I need something normal.

Mike

metadata.subject: Trying to process the last couple of weeks

artifacts.content:
Em,

Catching up between meetings and wanted to explain why I’m suddenly obsessed with this pickup replacement and, honestly, why I’ve been more on edge.

You remember how Dad’s oncology follow-up at Essentia–St. Joseph’s on 1/12/26 already had everyone stressed. Afterward, the old F-150 died in the parking lot and we sat there in subzero wind chills for over an hour waiting on a jump and a tow. It reminded me way too much of when Unit 23 stalled on Highway 25 on February 3, 2026, right after my cardiology follow‑up with Dr. Steven Kline for the angioplasty I had back in November 2025. That was the third time in six months I’d been stranded heading to or from heart appointments.

On top of that, Dad’s bypass is now set for June 10, 2026 at St. Joe’s. Last time he was in, after that minor heart attack in October 2023, I was out of the office for 11 workdays and a bunch of routine fleet decisions backed up. I’m trying to get Unit 23 ordered and out the door before June so his surgery and Mom’s care don’t delay things again.

I vented to my therapist, Dr. Karen Olson at Lake Country Behavioral Health, about it last week. She keeps connecting my panic spells to these late‑night breakdowns during call‑outs. Between that, the Essentia stall, and Dad’s parking lot episode, it’s not exactly helping the blood pressure.

Kids are okay—Caleb’s knee is holding up after the ACL surgery at Cuyuna, and Lauren’s still doing choir and seeing Dr. Porter. We’re juggling their schedules like always.

Call when you can.

Mike

metadata.subject: Why I’m pushing so hard on the truck (and Dad)

artifacts.content:
Lisa,

I wanted to give you a bit more context before the next agenda packet goes out and also own my part in why this pickup item is landing on your desk later than it should.

The truth is, this has been on my radar for a couple of years, but I kept kicking the can because we’ve had bigger fires to put out in Public Works and I honestly didn’t think Council would want to wrestle with another equipment request until something actually broke. I also only realized last week that the way we’ve been handling day‑to‑day use of Unit 23 technically doesn’t line up with some of the ordinance language on fleet assignments, so that’s on me for not catching it sooner.

I know this all sits in the shadow of the December 12, 2024 plow debacle—three hydraulics failing at once, the emergency one‑week rental from RDO Equipment for $4,750, and the corrective action plan that ended up in my 2025 review. You were clear that I can’t let replacements slip like that again if I expect to move up a step. Getting this truck on the March 2, 2026 agenda is part of me trying to show I heard you.

Internally, staff are split. A few folks think it’s not worth dragging Council through another vehicle discussion, but I’d rather get a clear decision and move on than keep limping along.

Appreciate your patience.

Mike

Personal planning notes – fleet / family / backlog

1) Unit 23 incidents to remember (for my own head, not to drag into meetings):
- October 7, 2025: Homecoming parade on Kingwood Street by the BHS stadium. Mark was supervising the closure while Caleb’s drumline was lining up. Overhead beacon and left taillight cut out again just as the buses were unloading. Lisa McDermott from the PTO cornered me about us parking a “rusted‑out, unsafe truck” around the kids.
- November 5, 2025: After a 12‑hour snow response, I backed into that parked Subaru on Laurel turning into our driveway at 1413 Spruce. Backup camera flickered off right as I was lining up. Reported it to the city’s risk manager, did the post‑incident screening at Cuyuna the next morning, and paid the $500 deductible myself so it wouldn’t ding our loss history.
- December 10, 2025: Dad slid into the streetlight by Gregory Park on Laurel. Called out at 11:45 p.m. for barricades, but we couldn’t safely tow the message board or salt spreader with Unit 23 because of the frame and brake line issues, so response was about 40 minutes slower.

2) Alternatives I’ve toyed with but don’t love:
- Lean more on Parks & Rec’s half‑ton and stage it at 1413 on mornings when I have PT at Essentia–Brainerd for my back. That would let me go straight from PT to inspections, but it would also broadcast my back issues to half the building.

3) I haven’t really built a Plan B if Council says no beyond “keep doing what we’ve been doing,” and I know that’s led to some inconsistent decisions by the crews. For the agenda packet, probably safest phrasing is something like: “Move to authorize the project as presented, with the understanding that we may need to come back later for additional funding if my preliminary estimates turn out to be low.” That threads the needle between being honest about the numbers and not spooking anyone on the finance side.

Tom,

Quick brain dump between site checks.

I’ve been kicking around alternatives in my head in case Council balks at the new pickup, and one idea that crossed my mind was leaning harder on your half‑ton. For example, on mornings when I’ve got PT at Essentia–Brainerd for my lower back, it would be convenient if your crew could stage your truck at my place on Spruce so I could go straight from PT to inspections without doubling back to SE 13th. But I realize that would probably scramble your schedule and, frankly, I’m not eager to advertise my back issues to the whole Parks crew.

The reality is we haven’t spent a ton of time building out elaborate alternatives. Everyone’s stretched thin, and I don’t have capacity to manage multiple scenarios while we’re also gearing up for County 3 and the downtown mill and overlay. If Council picks the “do nothing this year” route, we’ll just keep fielding complaints as they come in—most residents are used to the current conditions anyway—and I’ll keep juggling rides when Unit 23 is down.

I’m also still a little gun‑shy after that December 2024 rental from RDO and the note in my 2025 review, so I’m trying not to overcomplicate this. Just get a reliable half‑ton in place before next winter.

If you see any glaring impacts on your side I’m not thinking about, let me know.

Mike

Event: Cardiology follow‑up w/ Dr. Steven Kline
Date/Time: Tuesday, February 3, 2026 – 9:00–9:45 a.m.
Location: Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s Medical Center, Brainerd – Cardiology Clinic
Attendees: Michael Hansen

Notes:
- Follow‑up to November 2025 angioplasty.
- Bring updated list of meds and questions about stress test results.
- Ask specifically about the three episodes where I was stranded in Unit 23 in the last six months (including today’s stall on Highway 25 on the way back from this appointment). Mention how my therapist, Dr. Karen Olson at Lake Country Behavioral Health, keeps tying these breakdowns during after‑hours call‑outs to the panic attacks.

Event: Mom – diabetes follow‑up
Date/Time: Thursday, March 6, 2025 – 2:30–3:30 p.m.
Location: St. Joseph’s Medical Center, Brainerd – Endocrinology
Attendees: Judith Hansen, Michael Hansen

Notes:
- Coordinate with snow call‑out schedule and truck reliability; most of my 2025 comp time and sick leave already eaten up covering emergencies while she was in and out of St. Joe’s Jan–Mar 2025.
- Try to avoid getting stuck on a last‑minute call if Unit 23 is acting up.

Event: Dad – bypass surgery
Date/Time: Wednesday, June 10, 2026 – 7:00 a.m. check‑in
Location: St. Joseph’s Medical Center, Brainerd – Cardiac Surgery
Attendees: Richard & Judith Hansen, Michael & Emily

Notes:
- Aim to have Unit 23 replacement ordered and auction scheduled before this date.

Jenna,

I wanted to give you a heads‑up about something that may affect Caleb’s evening availability over the next couple of months.

We’re still limping along with an old city pickup (Unit 23) that has a knack for dying at the worst times. During that big February 2025 blizzard, I had to call Caleb out of his evening shift at the Cub Foods on Washington so he could shuttle me between job sites when the truck went down. If Council drags their feet on the replacement again this year, there’s a real chance I’ll end up in the same boat—pulling him from a 4–6 hour shift at the last minute so I can get between plow routes or barricade locations.

I’m pushing hard to get the new truck authorized at the March 2, 2026 council meeting. Our City Administrator made it pretty clear after the December 12, 2024 plow mess and the $4,750 emergency rental from RDO that I can’t let another replacement slide, so I’m trying to stay ahead of it this time.

On the home front, we’re also juggling Lauren’s counseling appointments at Essentia–Brainerd with Dr. Porter after that January 17 near‑miss by the high school, plus my dad’s June 10 bypass surgery at St. Joe’s, so our family calendar is a bit of a circus.

I really appreciate how flexible you’ve been with Caleb. He likes working there, and I don’t want my job or our decrepit fleet to keep messing with his schedule and your staffing.

Thanks,
Michael Hansen

metadata.subject: Possible schedule disruptions for Caleb during snow events

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Hansen <mhansen@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Emily Sorenson <emily.sorenson@email.com>
Date: March 19, 2024
Subject: Dad, his license, and the truck

Emily,

I wanted to follow up after talking with Mom about Dad’s situation. With his second DUI in Crow Wing County earlier this month and the resulting driver’s license suspension, I know transportation is going to be a real headache for both of them over the next 6–9 months.

I’ll be honest: I actually caught myself running through some pretty bad “solutions” the other day. One alternative I considered, but ultimately rejected, was assigning the street foreman’s truck to Dad to use temporarily and having the foreman share a unit with another crew out of the Public Works garage on SE 13th Street. On paper it looked like it could work if we juggled schedules, but in reality it would really hurt how quickly the foreman can respond to field issues and snow events. That’s not fair to the crews or the residents, and it would be hard to justify if anyone ever asked why he didn’t have his own vehicle.

So that idea is off the table. I’ll plan on driving down more often and helping with rides to appointments, and we can look at a more formal ride schedule between you, me, and maybe a couple of their church friends. I just wanted you to know I’m thinking about it and trying to keep Dad’s DUI mess separate from my fleet decisions at work.

Michael

metadata.subject: Dad, his license, and the truck

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Hansen <mhansen@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Brainerd City Council <council@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: January 20, 2026
Subject: Draft motion language – replacement of Unit #23

Council Members,

Attached is the staff report for the proposed replacement of Unit #23, the 2011 Ford F-150 currently assigned to the street foreman. As you know, I’ve been tracking this unit’s mileage, repair history, and downtime closely as part of our fleet spreadsheet, and it has been a recurring topic in our morning briefings with the mechanic. In the early draft of this agenda request that I saved to the Public Works shared drive, I had simply written, “Move to approve the staff recommendation as outlined, even though this option is not the one most residents favored at the open house,” but after thinking about it over the weekend and talking briefly with the City Administrator, I decided it would be better to spell out the operational context more clearly for the council record.

Below is draft motion language for your consideration:

“Motion to authorize the Public Works Director, Michael Hansen, to purchase one (1) 2026 Chevrolet Silverado 1500 WT 4x4 regular cab pickup truck through the Minnesota State Cooperative Purchasing Venture (CPV) contract from Nereson Chevrolet at a total cost not to exceed $37,850.00, including tax and estimated delivery charges; to approve the expenditure from the 2026 Equipment Replacement Fund as identified in the approved Capital Improvement Plan; and to declare the existing 2011 Ford F-150 (Unit #23, VIN ending in 4827) as surplus property and authorize its disposal via public auction, with sale proceeds returned to the Equipment Replacement Fund, noting that this replacement is time-sensitive because the current truck broke down on January 12, 2026 while I was transporting my father, Richard Hansen, from his oncology appointment at Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s Medical Center in Brainerd and left us stranded for over an hour in subzero temperatures. While staff have not yet completed the full life-cycle cost analysis or gathered all of the typical public input we’d include for a larger capital item, I would still recommend that the council move to approve this item now due to the operational and safety concerns tied to further delay.”

I recognize the personal nature of that last clause, but I felt it was important context for why I’m emphasizing reliability and timeliness on this replacement. If you would prefer more neutral language, or if you’d like me to soften the reference to the incomplete cost analysis and outreach, I can revise accordingly before the agenda packet is finalized.

Please let me know if you have questions or suggested edits.

Michael Hansen
Public Works Director
City of Brainerd

metadata.subject: Draft motion language – replacement of Unit #23

artifacts.content:
Personal Note – October 6, 2025
From: Michael Robert Hansen

I need to get my head straight about Friday before I meet with HR and the City Administrator.

On October 3, 2025, I was driving the 2011 Ford F-150 (Unit #23, VIN ending in 4827) eastbound on Washington Street, heading toward Brainerd High School after Lauren texted that she was having another anxiety attack between classes. At the intersection of Washington Street and South 7th Street in Brainerd, I glanced down to read her message instead of waiting for the next red light. In that split second, traffic in front of me stopped and I caused a minor at-fault collision.

No one was hurt, but the front end of the truck took another hit and the responding officer correctly listed me as distracted by a text. I’m sick about the fact that I let my concern for Lauren override basic driving sense, especially while operating a city vehicle as the Public Works Director. I’m worried about how this will look to the Council and what it means for my credibility on safety.

I’ve been drafting motion language for the eventual replacement of Unit #23—similar to the 2026 Chevrolet Silverado 1500 WT 4x4 proposal at around $37,850.00 from Nereson Chevrolet—but I need to be careful not to frame that request as me trying to bury this incident. Lauren’s anxiety and my mistake are separate from the fleet needs, even if they collided—literally—at Washington and 7th.

Michael
